segments.text: Just let everyone file in and then we'll wrap into it.
Alright, well, good afternoon, minister.
Welcome along to the Foreign Affairs Defense
and Trade Committee for this afternoon's hearing
on Vote Customs.
Uh, we have an hour set down for today's hearing.
We will hand over to you for some introductory comments,
and then I'm sure there'll be a range of questions.
Thank you very much. I'd first like
to acknowledge the customs officials here today
to help me in this role, but thank you, Mr.
Chair. Um, I think just to set the scene, um, customs,
as you know, um, is responsible for the collection
of revenue supporting trade, protecting our border,
and facilitating travel.
So they are a very busy unit.
Um, I'll briefly comment on a couple
of areas I'm sure the committee will want to ask more about.
Um, first of all, customs is stopping an unprecedented
quantity of illegal drugs nearly five tons
in the past 12 months.
Over the past 10 years, seizures
of methamphetamine have increased nearly tenfold to
around 2,500 kilograms in 2024 calendar year.
The pattern is just as stark when looking at interceptions
of meth destined for New Zealand offshore seizures have gone
from 10 kilograms in 2016 to
around 2,300 kilograms last year.
Cocaine seizures are smaller in scale compared
to methamphetamine, but have significantly
increased in recent years.
Just last month, customs seized almost 300 kilograms
of cocaine of two ships that arrived at the port
of toga via Panama.
These patterns are part of the context
for the government supporting customs
with an extra 35 million in this year's budget,
this will target more offenders involved in smaller
to medium quantity, high frequency drug smuggling,
who we know aspire to be the big players in the future.
The funding also aims to boost the ability to identify
and prosecute those trusted insiders who use their access
and ports or airports to facilitate criminal activity
and will allow customs
to put additional effort into helping raise awareness
with the private sector partners
and build a broader response to organized crime.
The extra money will also fund an additional overseas
customs liaison officer adding to the agency's network
of international intelligence sharing capabilities
that help stop drugs reaching New Zealand.
A lot of work is going on
around improving the broader government response
to transnational serious and organized crime
and customs is providing the secretariat support
to the trans transnational
and serious organized Crime Ministerial Advisory Group,
which I'm happy to discuss later.
While border protection is understandably a major focus,
there are other significant pieces of work underway.
One of the most important of these is the review
of customs fees to recover the cost of managing
and processing goods across our border.
Changes being made to these fees
and the structure of the fees regime will ensure costs are
recovered more fairly sustainably,
and remove taxpayer subsidization.
New rates for goods management fees will take effect from
one July with structural changes happening from
one April next year.
Another piece of work in the area of customs trade,
which is happening towards the end of this year,
is a new time release survey
that measures customs clearance times for goods.
The last survey was carried out in 2023
and showed customs cleared nearly 94%
of sea cargo containers before arrival in New Zealand.
Over 78%
of air cargo imports entries were cleared before arrival.
Reflecting the much shorter time,
time travels or air freight.
A hundred percent of air cargo exports were cleared
before leaving New Zealand.
One more example of the high quality service customs
delivers and its commitment to protecting, um, commitment
to protect and promote New Zealand's interests across the
borders, whether to take any questions.
Excellent. Thank you Council.
Jenny Solicitor minister,
your ministerial advisory group on transnational, um,
serious and organized crime.

by record drug imports.

but we also see corruption, according to your ministerial,
uh, advisory group's report, insider threats
failed interagency coordination.

losing the fight against transnational organized crime?
And if not, why?

around the commentary from the reports.

to be bold to be, um, an analytical in its approach.
Um, we have, um,
and I think if you put the reports in context
around the challenges that we are facing,
it is not talking about customs failing.
It's talking about New Zealand's response
overall in the world's climate.

how do the agencies connect together better?
How do they work more cooperatively and what more can we do?

work policing customs are doing.
How we are seizing more than we've ever seized before.

that are targeting New Zealand, and
therefore the focus of the group is what do we
as a country do to be better at it?

that we are not delivering.
I think we can name numerous operations that the customs
and police and other agencies have done.
What we know is we can do more and we can do better.
And from my position,
I believe New Zealand should be the very best in the world
at this because we have the advantage of a single border.
We have a geographic isolation,
we have a single enforcement agencies,
we have a single jurisdiction.
So therefore, how we connect up and what we do,
and that's what the ministerial Advisory Group has been set
up to do, is to challenge what we're doing now, analyze
what we're doing now, and identify what we can do better.
